tasks.prompt: [Warm-up] Use Pod Security Admission to create a 'psa-test' namespace and apply the 'restricted' standard in warn mode. Then attempt to create a Pod with a privileged container (privileged: true) in that namespace and observe the warning message.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Create the namespace
kubectl create namespace psa-test

# 2. Apply the restricted standard in warn mode (not yet blocking)
kubectl label namespace psa-test \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=___

# 3. Verify the namespace label
kubectl get namespace psa-test --show-labels

# 4. Attempt to create a privileged Pod (warning expected)
kubectl apply -n psa-test -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: priv-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: test
    image: nginx
    securityContext:
      privileged: true
EOF
tasks.solution:
# 1. Create namespace
kubectl create namespace psa-test

# 2. Apply warn mode with restricted standard
kubectl label namespace psa-test \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=restricted

# 3. Verify label
kubectl get namespace psa-test --show-labels
# NAME       STATUS   AGE   LABELS
# psa-test   Active   10s   kubernetes.io/metadata.name=psa-test,pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=restricted

# 4. Attempt to create a privileged Pod
kubectl apply -n psa-test -f priv-pod.yaml
# Warning: would violate PodSecurity "restricted:latest":
#   privileged (container "test" must not set securityContext.privileged=true),
#   allowPrivilegeEscalation != false (container "test" must set securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation=false),
#   unrestricted capabilities (container "test" must set securityContext.capabilities.drop=["ALL"]),
#   runAsNonRoot != true (pod or container "test" must set securityContext.runAsNonRoot=true),
#   seccompProfile (pod or container "test" must set securityContext.seccompProfile.type to "RuntimeDefault" or "Localhost")
# pod/priv-pod created   <-- created because we're only in warn mode

# 5. Delete the Pod
kubectl delete pod priv-pod -n psa-test
tasks.explanation: Pod Security Admission (PSA) is the built-in admission controller that replaced PodSecurityPolicy in Kubernetes 1.25+. It combines three standards (privileged/baseline/restricted) with three modes (enforce/audit/warn), applied via namespace labels. In warn mode, violations produce a warning but the resource is still created. Starting with warn to understand the blast radius is the recommended approach when gradually tightening policies.
hints: You can set all three PSA modes simultaneously. Example: pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=baseline, pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=restricted
First confirm the Kubernetes version supports PSA (1.23 alpha, 1.25 stable): kubectl version --short
Adding the enforce label to a namespace where Pods are already running does not affect existing Pods — only new Pod creation is blocked.

tasks.prompt: [Core Practice] Install Kyverno via Helm and create a ClusterPolicy in enforce mode that requires all Pods to have an 'app' label. Verify that Pods without the label are blocked and Pods with it are allowed.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Install Kyverno
helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno/
helm repo update
helm install kyverno kyverno/kyverno -n kyverno --create-namespace

# 2. Wait for Kyverno Pods to be Ready
kubectl get pods -n kyverno -w

# 3. Write the ClusterPolicy (enforce mode)
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-app-label
spec:
  validationFailureAction: ___   # Enforce or Audit
  rules:
    - name: check-app-label
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Pod must have an 'app' label."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: "?*"    # requires a non-empty value

# 4. Attempt to create a Pod without a label (should be blocked)
kubectl run no-label-pod --image=nginx

# 5. Create a Pod with a label (should be allowed)
kubectl run labeled-pod --image=nginx -l app=web
tasks.solution:
# 1~2. Install Kyverno (run the commands above)
# Wait 1–2 minutes

# 3. Create the ClusterPolicy
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-app-label
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Enforce
  rules:
    - name: check-app-label
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Pod must have an 'app' label."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: "?*"
EOF
# clusterpolicy.kyverno.io/require-app-label created

# 4. Pod without label → blocked
kubectl run no-label-pod --image=nginx
# Error from server: admission webhook "validate.kyverno.svc-fail" denied the request:
# resource Pod/default/no-label-pod was blocked due to the following policies
# require-app-label:
#   check-app-label: Pod must have an 'app' label.

# 5. Pod with label → allowed
kubectl run labeled-pod --image=nginx -l app=web
# pod/labeled-pod created

kubectl get pod labeled-pod --show-labels
# NAME          READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE   LABELS
# labeled-pod   1/1     Running   0          10s   app=web
tasks.explanation: Kyverno is a Kubernetes-native policy engine that declares policies as CRD-based YAML. With validationFailureAction: Enforce, the Validating Admission Webhook rejects violating requests directly at the API server. With Audit, violations are recorded but creation is allowed, which is useful for incrementally rolling out policies. Unlike OPA Gatekeeper, Kyverno requires no Rego — policies are written as YAML patterns, lowering the barrier to entry.
hints: Applying a policy before the Kyverno webhook is fully ready may produce 'connection refused' errors. Ensure all Pods in the kyverno namespace are Running before proceeding.
Use a namespaced Policy instead of ClusterPolicy to apply the rule only to a specific namespace.
Review the policy violation history with kubectl get policyreport -A.

tasks.prompt: [Core Practice] Write a Kyverno mutate policy that automatically injects an 'app' label into Pods that are created without one. Apply the policy, create a Pod without a label, and confirm the label was injected automatically.
tasks.starter:
# Mutate policy: auto-inject app label
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-default-app-label
spec:
  rules:
    - name: add-app-label
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Pod
      mutate:
        patchStrategicMerge:
          metadata:
            labels:
              +(app): ___    # '+()' adds the value only if the field is absent

# First delete or set the existing enforce policy to Audit
kubectl delete clusterpolicy require-app-label

# Apply the mutate policy
kubectl apply -f mutate-policy.yaml

# Create a Pod without a label
kubectl run auto-labeled --image=nginx

# Verify the label was injected automatically
kubectl get pod auto-labeled --show-labels
tasks.solution:
# 1. Delete the existing enforce policy
kubectl delete clusterpolicy require-app-label

# 2. Apply the mutate policy
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-default-app-label
spec:
  rules:
    - name: add-app-label
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Pod
      mutate:
        patchStrategicMerge:
          metadata:
            labels:
              +(app): default
EOF

# 3. Create a Pod without a label
kubectl run auto-labeled --image=nginx
# pod/auto-labeled created  (no rejection this time)

# 4. Confirm the label was injected automatically
kubectl get pod auto-labeled --show-labels
# NAME           READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE   LABELS
# auto-labeled   1/1     Running   0          5s    app=default  <-- auto-injected!

# 5. Clean up
kubectl delete pod labeled-pod auto-labeled
kubectl delete clusterpolicy add-default-app-label
tasks.explanation: A Mutating Admission Webhook can modify the request body before a resource is persisted to etcd. Kyverno's +(field) syntax is a conditional patch that adds a value only when the field is absent — it does not overwrite an existing value. Pods that already have an app label are left untouched; the default is injected only when the label is missing. While a Validating Webhook allows or denies requests, a Mutating Webhook transforms them to automatically satisfy policies. In production this is used for sidecar injection, setting default resource values, and adding common labels.
hints: The +(field) syntax in patchStrategicMerge means conditional addition — it preserves existing values and only fills in the missing ones.
Mutate and validate policies can coexist; mutate runs first and then validate inspects the result.
To verify that mutation occurred, run kubectl get pod auto-labeled -o yaml | grep -A5 'labels:' to inspect the full metadata.

tasks.prompt: [Application] Apply the 'restricted' Pod Security Admission standard in enforce mode to the psa-test namespace. Then write a security-hardened Pod that fully complies with the restricted standard (non-root, capabilities drop ALL, seccompProfile RuntimeDefault) and verify it is created successfully.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Add enforce mode to the existing psa-test namespace
kubectl label namespace psa-test \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted \
  --overwrite

# 2. Write a Pod YAML compliant with the restricted standard
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-pod
  namespace: psa-test
  labels:
    app: secure
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: ___
    seccompProfile:
      type: ___
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:alpine
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: ___
      capabilities:
        drop:
          - ___
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8080

# 3. Apply and verify
kubectl apply -f secure-pod.yaml
tasks.solution:
# 1. Apply enforce mode
kubectl label namespace psa-test \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted \
  --overwrite

# 2. Compliant Pod
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-pod
  namespace: psa-test
  labels:
    app: secure
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 1000
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:alpine
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      capabilities:
        drop:
          - ALL
EOF
# pod/secure-pod created  (passes restricted standard)

kubectl get pod secure-pod -n psa-test
# NAME         READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# secure-pod   1/1     Running   0          10s

# 3. Confirm enforce blocking with a non-compliant Pod
kubectl run blocked-pod --image=nginx -n psa-test
# Error from server (Forbidden): pods "blocked-pod" is forbidden:
#   violates PodSecurity "restricted:latest":
#   allowPrivilegeEscalation != false, ...

# 4. Clean up
kubectl delete pod secure-pod -n psa-test
kubectl delete namespace psa-test
tasks.explanation: The restricted standard is the most stringent security level in Kubernetes PSA. Its key requirements are: runAsNonRoot: true (forbid root execution), allowPrivilegeEscalation: false (block privilege escalation), capabilities.drop: [ALL] (remove all Linux capabilities), and seccompProfile.type: RuntimeDefault or Localhost (restrict syscalls). Setting all four fields minimizes the container's blast radius on the host kernel. Unlike warn/audit, enforce mode completely blocks non-compliant Pod creation.
hints: The nginx image runs as root by default and may fail the restricted standard. Specify runAsUser: 1000 with nginx:alpine, or use the nginx:unprivileged image.
The recommended pattern after 'capabilities.drop: [ALL]' is to add back only the capabilities the service actually needs. For example, NET_BIND_SERVICE is required for ports below 1024.
The full requirements of each PSA standard are documented at kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
